title: Polynesian settlement
summary: The ancestors of Māori settle Aotearoa.
what: Polynesian navigators established communities that developed distinct iwi, hapū, language and tikanga.
why: Māori sovereignty and biculturalism are foundational, not later additions to New Zealand history.
colle: What does it mean to recognise Indigenous sovereignty in a modern state?

title: Abel Tasman reaches New Zealand
summary: The first recorded European encounter is violent.
what: Tasman’s expedition sighted the islands and clashed with Māori in Golden Bay.
why: The encounter began European mapping without establishing control.
colle: Why do “discovery” narratives erase Indigenous knowledge?

title: Cook maps the islands
summary: British knowledge and imperial interest expand.
what: James Cook circumnavigated and mapped New Zealand while engaging in both exchange and conflict with Māori.
why: His voyages prepared later trade, missionary activity and colonisation.
colle: Can exploration ever be politically neutral?

title: The Treaty of Waitangi
summary: The foundational agreement is signed in English and Māori.
what: Many rangatira signed versions that differed over whether sovereignty or governance was ceded.
why: Conflicting texts and repeated breaches remain central to law, politics and national identity.
colle: Can a state be legitimate when its founding treaty is disputed?

title: The New Zealand Wars
summary: Conflict accompanies land acquisition and Crown expansion.
what: British and colonial forces fought Māori movements resisting loss of land and authority.
why: Confiscations created grievances that later Treaty settlements sought to address.
colle: How should a country commemorate wars once excluded from national memory?

title: Māori parliamentary seats
summary: Reserved representation is created.
what: Four Māori electorates were established, initially as a temporary measure.
why: The seats provided representation but also reflected unequal assumptions about property and citizenship.
colle: Do reserved seats strengthen equality or institutionalise difference?

title: Women’s suffrage
summary: New Zealand becomes the first self-governing country where women vote nationally.
what: A mass campaign led by figures including Kate Sheppard secured the vote for women.
why: The achievement became a durable symbol of progressive national identity.
colle: Why did New Zealand lead the world on women’s suffrage?

title: Dominion status
summary: New Zealand becomes a dominion within the British Empire.
what: The constitutional label changed while strong political and cultural ties to Britain remained.
why: It illustrates the gradual rather than revolutionary path to independence.
colle: When does a colony truly become independent?

title: Gallipoli and ANZAC
summary: Shared sacrifice with Australia becomes a national myth.
what: New Zealand troops suffered heavy casualties in the failed Gallipoli campaign.
why: ANZAC memory strengthened national consciousness while remaining intertwined with Empire.
colle: Does wartime sacrifice provide a healthy basis for national identity?

title: Full legislative independence
summary: Parliament adopts the Statute of Westminster.
what: New Zealand accepted full legislative autonomy from Britain.
why: The late adoption reflected enduring attachment to Britain and the absence of a dramatic independence struggle.
colle: Why was New Zealand slow to formalise independence?

title: The Waitangi Tribunal and Land March
summary: Māori protest forces historical grievances onto the national agenda.
what: The Tribunal was created to examine Treaty claims, while the Land March opposed further alienation of Māori land.
why: The year marked a turning point in the Māori renaissance and the politics of redress.
colle: Can legal settlements repair cultural and territorial dispossession?

title: Rogernomics
summary: Rapid market reform transforms the economy.
what: The Labour government deregulated finance, cut subsidies and privatised state assets.
why: The reforms increased efficiency in some sectors but also unemployment, inequality and debate over neoliberalism.
colle: Did market reform modernise New Zealand at too high a social cost?

title: Rainbow Warrior and the nuclear-free policy
summary: New Zealand asserts an independent foreign policy.
what: French agents sank the Greenpeace ship Rainbow Warrior, while nuclear-free legislation restricted visits by nuclear-capable vessels.
why: The dispute with France and the United States made anti-nuclear policy a badge of national independence.
colle: Can a small country influence global security norms?

title: Te reo Māori becomes an official language
summary: The state recognises the Māori language.
what: The Māori Language Act granted official status and created institutions to support revitalisation.
why: Language revival became central to biculturalism and decolonisation.
colle: Can official status reverse language decline?

title: MMP electoral reform
summary: Voters replace first-past-the-post with proportional representation.
what: A referendum adopted Mixed Member Proportional representation, first used in 1996.
why: MMP diversified Parliament and made coalition government normal.
colle: Does proportional representation improve democratic legitimacy?

title: The Christchurch earthquake
summary: A major disaster reshapes the country’s second-largest city.
what: The earthquake killed 185 people and caused extensive destruction.
why: Recovery raised questions about resilience, insurance, urban planning and centralised decision-making.
colle: How should governments balance speed and participation after disaster?

title: Jacinda Ardern becomes Prime Minister
summary: A new style of progressive leadership gains global attention.
what: Ardern formed a coalition government and emphasised empathy, child poverty reduction and wellbeing.
why: Her leadership became a global symbol while domestic debates continued over housing, inequality and delivery.
colle: Does political empathy translate into effective government?

title: The Christchurch mosque attacks
summary: White-supremacist terrorism kills 51 worshippers.
what: A gunman attacked two mosques; the government rapidly tightened firearms laws and promoted social solidarity.
why: The response was praised internationally but also exposed Islamophobia and online extremism.
colle: Can decisive gun reform prevent future violence?

title: The COVID-19 response
summary: An elimination strategy wins global attention.
what: Strict border controls, lockdowns and public-health communication initially kept deaths low.
why: The strategy reinforced the image of competent small-state government but later produced fatigue and polarisation.
colle: Why did pandemic consensus eventually weaken?

title: National returns to power
summary: Christopher Luxon forms a three-party coalition.
what: National governed with ACT and New Zealand First after Labour’s support fell sharply.
why: The change brought a more conservative programme and renewed conflict over Māori policy, public spending and the Treaty.
colle: Is New Zealand entering a more polarised political era?

title: The Treaty Principles Bill debate
summary: A proposed rewrite of Treaty principles triggers mass opposition and is defeated.
what: ACT’s bill passed a first reading amid a major hīkoi but was rejected at its second reading in April 2025.
why: The controversy revealed deep disagreement over equality, partnership, Māori rights and the meaning of the Treaty.
colle: Can one legal definition settle the meaning of the Treaty of Waitangi?
